serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of sliced fish beehoon soup with milk is approximately 500 g, as typically served at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Sliced fish beehoon soup (with milk) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Sliced fish beehoon soup (with milk)', CR ID F05060119. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (cooked) — Glycemic Index entry; analogous estimate applied to sliced fish beehoon soup with milk
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for sliced fish beehoon soup with milk as a composite dish. GI of 53 is estimated from cooked rice vermicelli (beehoon) as the dominant carbohydrate source, per University of Sydney GI Database. The presence of fish protein and evaporated milk in the broth further moderates the glycaemic response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (53) × carbohydrate per serving (47.5 g from 500 g serving at 9.5 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 25.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 260 mg/100g (1300 mg per 500 g bowl) approaches the daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals, particularly when broth is consumed in full.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt in the broth, avoid adding soy sauce or high-sodium condiments, and leave a significant portion of the soup broth to limit cumulative sodium intake to within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli (beehoon) has a moderate GI (~53); carbohydrate at 9.5 g/100g is moderate, and sugar is low at 0.7 g/100g; fish protein and evaporated milk help moderate postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should control portion size of the rice vermicelli, confirm that evaporated rather than sweetened condensed milk is used, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to further blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is low at 0.8 g/100g with estimated saturated fat of ~0.3 g/100g; however, sodium at 260 mg/100g is a cardiovascular concern at typical serving sizes, and the omega-3 content from sliced fish provides a modest cardioprotective benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose this dish for its low fat and omega-3 content from fish, but must manage sodium intake by limiting broth consumption and avoiding additional high-sodium condiments.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sliced fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at ~0.3 g/100g; evaporated milk contributes calcium beneficial for RA-related bone protection.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of the fish, but should request reduced salt to keep sodium below 200 mg/100g and avoid nightshade-based condiments that may trigger flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sliced Fish Beehoon Soup with Milk is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of fresh sliced white fish (commonly batang, snapper, or grouper), thin rice vermicelli noodles (beehoon), leafy vegetables, and a light fish-based broth enriched with evaporated milk to create a creamy, milky white soup. The dish is typically prepared by simmering a fish bone stock, adding sliced fish pieces that cook quickly in the hot broth, and finishing with evaporated milk for a mild, slightly sweet creaminess. Ginger slices are commonly added to neutralise any fishy aroma. The texture is soft and comforting, with tender fish, silky noodles, and a smooth, lightly savoury broth. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories at 51 kcal/100g, moderate in carbohydrate at 9.5 g/100g from the rice vermicelli, low in fat at 0.8 g/100g, and provides 2.96 g/100g of protein from the fish and milk. Sodium is 260 mg/100g, which is borderline for hypertensive individuals at a full 500 g serving. The fish contributes omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective benefits. The evaporated milk adds calcium and a modest amount of saturated fat. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 53 (low) based on the rice vermicelli component, moderated by the protein and fat content of the dish. This dish is widely consumed as a lunch or dinner option at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts, and is considered a lighter, health-conscious noodle soup choice compared to richer broth-based dishes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05060119) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food identity is exact. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous source (University of Sydney GI Database, cooked rice vermicelli) with documented basis.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at ~0.3 g/100g based on the low total fat of 0.8 g/100g and the known fat profile of white fish and evaporated milk; HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA for this specific composite preparation. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under 'western' cuisine and applied 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' dietary tags; these have been corrected to 'Singaporean' cuisine with appropriate dietary flags (is_vegetarian: false, is_vegan: false, is_halal: true). The input region_tag of 'malaysia' has been corrected to 'Singapore' as this is a canonical Singaporean hawker dish. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from cooked rice vermicelli per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite-dish GI measurement is available in approved sources.
